external.description: The U.S. Department of Justice (DOJ) has escalated efforts to uncover the identities of anonymous social media users who have criticized immigration enforcement, specifically the operations of Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE). The U.S. Attorney's Office for Washington has issued grand jury subpoenas to platforms including Reddit and X, requesting personal information such as names, addresses, and banking details of at least two users who posted critiques of ICE activities. Initially, the DOJ attempted to obtain this information via an administrative summons, which does not indicate a criminal investigation, but has now escalated to a grand jury subpoena, signaling possible criminal proceedings. Defense attorneys for the targeted individuals suggest the charges could involve the disclosure of federal officer locations or perceived threats, though the social media posts themselves do not indicate violent intent. Civil liberties advocates have raised concerns that these tactics could undermine protections for anonymous speech, citing historical precedent such as The Federalist Papers. Motions to quash these subpoenas are currently pending before U.S. District Chief Judge James Boasberg, and the outcome could have significant implications for online speech and privacy rights.
external.title: DOJ Issues Subpoenas to Identify Anonymous Social Media Users Criticizing ICE

19th-Century Russian Kokoshnik Headdress and Its Cultural Significance

🤖 IA: It's not clickbait ✅
👥 Users: It's not clickbait ✅

#kokoshnik #russianfashion #19thcentury 

View full AI summary:

external.description:
The article explores the historical and cultural significance of the kokoshnik, a traditional Russian women’s headdress widely associated with late 19th-century fashion and earlier centuries of Slavic heritage. The kokoshnik is described as a crest-shaped ornamental headpiece typically worn with the sarafan, a traditional jumper-style dress. Its origins can be traced back as early as the 10th century in Veliky Novgorod. The name derives from the Old Slavic word “kokosh,” meaning hen or cockerel, inspired by the headdress’s resemblance to a bird’s crest.

Historically, the kokoshnik held strong social and cultural meaning. It was primarily reserved for married women, reflecting Orthodox Christian customs in which married women were expected to keep their hair covered in public. Unmarried girls instead wore a similar but open-backed version known as a povyazka. The kokoshnik functioned not only as a garment but also as a marker of identity, signaling a woman’s marital status, regional origin, and social class. Each design could communicate subtle information about the wearer’s background.

The construction of authentic kokoshniks was highly skilled and resource-intensive. They were typically built on a rigid base made from birch bark or thick cardboard and covered with luxurious materials such as silk, velvet, or damask. Decorative elements often included pearls, mother-of-pearl, gold embroidery, and precious stones, making them valuable family heirlooms passed down through generations.

By the late 19th century, the kokoshnik remained an important symbol of Russian cultural heritage, reflecting both aesthetic traditions and social structures of the time. The article emphasizes its role as both a fashion object and a cultural artifact.
external.title: 19th-Century Russian Kokoshnik Headdress and Its Cultural Significance

external.description:
Amazon Connect Customer now supports tag-based access controls for the agent login/logout report, enabling you to apply granular access controls to meet compliance and regulatory requirements for data access. Contact center administrators can use resource tags to control who has access to view login and logout information for specific agents in their contact center. For example, you can tag agents with Department:Customer Service to only enable the Customer Service team manager to see login/logout information for these agents.
 
 Amazon Connect Customer agent login/logout report is available in all AWS commercial and AWS GovCloud (US-West) https://aws.amazon.com/about-aws/global-infrastructure/regional-product-services/ where Amazon Connect Customer is offered. To learn more about dashboards, see the . To learn more about Amazon Connect Customer, the AWS cloud-based contact center, please visit the https://aws.amazon.com/connect/.

record.text:
AWS IoT Core now supports direct messaging for point-to-point communication

https://aws.amazon.com/iot-core/ now supports the ability to send point-to-point messages to any connected device, providing better visibility into message delivery and lower messaging cost. AWS IoT Core is a fully...

#AWS

external.description:
https://aws.amazon.com/iot-core/ now supports the ability to send point-to-point messages to any connected device, providing better visibility into message delivery and lower messaging cost. AWS IoT Core is a fully managed service that securely connects IoT devices to the AWS cloud, and enables bi-directional messaging between IoT devices and cloud services.
 
 Previously, sending messages to a single IoT device required publishing to a topic subscribed by the device, with no built-in way to confirm delivery from the receiving device. With the https://docs.aws.amazon.com/iot/latest/developerguide/direct-messaging.html, you can send a message directly to any device connected to AWS IoT Core, and opt-in to receive delivery acknowledgement from the device. AWS IoT Core also uses the delivery acknowledgement to provide detailed API response codes and emit Amazon CloudWatch Logs, giving you visibility into message delivery status and failure reasons.
 
 Direct messaging is available in all AWS Regions where AWS IoT Core is available, including Amazon China and AWS GovCloud (US). To get started, see the direct messaging https://docs.aws.amazon.com/iot/latest/developerguide/direct-messaging.html. For pricing details, visit the AWS IoT Core https://aws.amazon.com/iot-core/pricing/.
external.title: AWS IoT Core now supports direct messaging for point-to-point communication

record.text:
Amazon WorkSpaces Applications adds support for Windows Desktop OS

Amazon WorkSpaces Applications now supports the ability to set up streaming resources powered by Windows Desktop operating systems using Bring Your Own License (BYOL). Customers can now bri...

#AWS #AwsGovcloudUs #AmazonAppstream20

external.description:
Amazon WorkSpaces Applications now supports the ability to set up streaming resources powered by Windows Desktop operating systems using Bring Your Own License (BYOL). Customers can now bring their existing Windows Desktop licenses to support their eligible Microsoft 365 Apps for enterprise, delivering a consistent and familiar desktop experience as users move between on-premises and virtual desktop environments.
 
 With BYOL support on WorkSpaces Applications, the operating system is hosted on hardware dedicated to the customer's AWS account, enabling customers to stream Windows desktop applications and full desktop experiences at scale. Customers benefit from cost savings by bringing their existing Windows Desktop OS licenses, eliminating OS fees so they only pay for compute and streaming infrastructure. When the local device and the streaming session both run the same Windows Desktop OS, users apply the same workflows, shortcuts, and navigation in both environments. This removes the cognitive overhead of adapting to a different desktop experience when switching between local and remote work, reducing onboarding time.
 
 Windows Desktop for WorkSpaces Applications is available in multiple AWS Regions. For the list of supported regions, see https://docs.aws.amazon.com/appstream2/latest/developerguide/byol-windows-images.html.
 
 To take advantage of BYOL on WorkSpaces Applications, organizations must meet Microsoft's licensing requirements and commit to running a minimum number of streaming resources in a given AWS Region each month. To learn more about eligibility requirements and getting started, see the https://docs.aws.amazon.com/appstream2/  and https://aws.amazon.com/windows/faq/.
external.title: Amazon WorkSpaces Applications adds support for Windows Desktop OS
